story:
Sarah climbed into the skiff, pushed off the dock, and fired up the engine as gasoline fumes rose and rain began to needle her coat. Ten minutes later, the drizzle turned to sheets, mist closing in like a curtain. She tugged at her collar and thought of Annalee—warm in bed at the cabin with the neighbor keeping watch—thought of how she would crawl beside her, say Mommy loves you and I’m sorry. After tonight things will change. This time, she meant it.

“I just want to go back to the cabin,” she said, pretending she’d left something under the couch, as if a lost thing could be a reason to turn around.

“Didn’t you check three times?” Edgar asked, the brim of his muddied Yankees cap askew, neck hair straggling over his raincoat collar. He had that grin he’d worn when they were kids, as if a secret pulsed between them that no one else could touch. “I want to show you something instead.”

A hard ripple slammed the hull. Sarah pitched forward, caught herself on the empty seat. A splinter lodged in her palm; blood beaded. She cursed and worried it free. “I should go home. We shouldn’t have done this.”

Edgar slid closer, hand grazing her leg. “It’s not far. Annalee’s asleep and safe. What’s a few more hours?”

“No,” she started, but he was already reaching past her for the tiller, already choosing for both of them.

They cut upriver, rain drumming, until a dark, low mountain rose ahead, a narrow break in its side. Edgar jumped into the mud and pulled her after him. He tied the boat to a thick tree. The river tugged at the rope like it wanted her back. His coat was missing buttons, his laces were undone, yet something in him had sharpened—focus, edged with a shimmer of fear.

“If I didn’t trust you, I’d be scared,” she said, leaning into him. She was scared anyway. The trees watched. The dark between them breathed.

“Follow me,” he said.

“Tell me where we’re going first.”

“You’ll be home soon. Back with Annalee.”

She tried once more. “We have to stop doing this—running off, pretending it fixes anything. I know this year—”

“Not here,” he snapped, softening it with a nod. “Come, or wait by the boat.”

She cast a glance at the heaving water, then at him. Sometimes she thought being with him would get her killed. He smiled. She smiled back. Something was off—excitement trembling over something colder, deeper. She placed a hand on his shoulder and went with him.

In the cave, rainwater ticked from stone. What little light remained thinned behind them, and the stink of the river said home was still possible—until it wasn’t. Time stretched: an hour, maybe more. Every time she asked how long, his answers got shorter until there were none. How could he want a place he couldn’t seem to find?

“We’re here,” he said at last, face half-shadowed, a thin edge in his voice.

He told her what to do. Hand to granite. Keep walking when the dark becomes total. If the wall vanishes, you’re close. The most important thing: do not stop.

“What happens if I stop?”

“I don’t want to think about that.”

“Why wouldn’t I hear you?”

“This isn’t a normal place. It might help you. It helps me.”

“Why do you want to be here?”

“Everyone has their reasons. Even you.”

Her hand trembled. She didn’t want this and couldn’t make herself leave. She pressed her palm to his shoulder, felt the strength there, and wanted the safety of it. Then the rock slid away beneath her fingers. The dark erased her arms. The drip of water died. Silence ballooned until her footsteps echoed inside her bones.

Worst of all, Edgar’s shoulder slipped out of her grasp. She called his name, once, then again, and the cave swallowed her voice. She remembered: don’t stop. “Not without you,” she whispered, but she kept moving anyway.

The voice that reached her then was not his. Rough, familiar. A voice she knew instantly.

“Please,” she said, knees hitting stone. She shut her eyes and folded into the dark.
